findings.title: N+1 query in conversations list endpoint
findings.what: The `conversations` query (lines 158-162) counts messages per conversation inside a Python loop, issuing N+1 SQL queries. Replace with a single query using `func.count()` with a subquery or LEFT JOIN + GROUP BY to get message counts in one roundtrip.
findings.why: Users with many conversations will see slow load times. This is the main list endpoint loaded on every app open — latency here directly impacts perceived performance.

findings.title: Remove dead diagram generation code paths
findings.what: Both backend (mutations.py line 501: `if False:`) and frontend (ChatInterface.tsx lines 238, 264: `if (false)`) have disabled diagram rendering behind dead code blocks with TODO comments. Extract these into a feature flag or remove entirely and track re-enablement as a separate issue.
findings.why: Dead code confuses contributors about what's actually functional. The `if False` / `if (false)` pattern bypasses linters and makes the codebase harder to reason about. Either commit to removing it or put it behind a proper feature toggle.

findings.title: Unused `requests` package in backend dependencies
findings.what: The `requests==2.31.0` package is listed in requirements.txt but `httpx` is used for all HTTP calls (OpenRouter API). Grep the backend for actual `import requests` usage — if none found, remove it to reduce Docker image size and dependency surface.
findings.why: A recent commit (7d30164) already removed a duplicate `requests` entry, suggesting awareness this may be vestigial. Keeping unused deps increases attack surface and image build time.

findings.title: Step-by-step guided pattern mode
findings.what: Add a 'Guided Mode' that takes a pattern's `translated_text` and splits it into numbered steps the user can tap through one at a time. Each step shows: the instruction, a stitch diagram thumbnail, stitch count, and a 'mark complete' checkbox. Backend: new mutation `parsePatternSteps(projectId)` that uses the AI to split translated text into a JSON array of steps. Frontend: new component with step navigation, progress bar, and per-step completion state persisted to the project.
findings.why: This is the #1 product gap. The app translates patterns but then dumps a wall of text. Beginners — the target users — need to follow patterns one row at a time while their hands are busy with yarn. A guided mode transforms Crooked Finger from a translation tool into a crafting companion. No competitor does this well.

findings.title: AI-powered pattern difficulty scoring
findings.what: The `CrochetProject` model already has a `difficulty_level` field but it's manually set and often empty. Add an `autoScoreDifficulty(projectId)` mutation that sends the pattern text to the AI with a structured prompt returning: difficulty (1-5), required techniques list, and a one-line rationale. Auto-trigger this on pattern creation. Frontend: show difficulty as colored badges in PatternLibrary and allow filtering/sorting by difficulty.
findings.why: Beginners don't know which patterns are appropriate for their skill level. Auto-scoring leverages the existing AI integration with minimal new code and makes the pattern library immediately more useful for discovery. The data model already supports it — this just fills the gap.

findings.title: Yarn quantity calculator from pattern analysis
findings.what: Add a `calculateYarnNeeds(projectId)` mutation that analyzes a pattern's stitch counts, dimensions, and yarn weight to estimate: total yardage needed, number of skeins (for common brands), and cost range. Return as a new `YarnEstimate` GraphQL type. The model already stores `yarn_weight` and `hook_size` — combine these with AI analysis of the pattern text to produce estimates. Frontend: show as a card on ProjectDetailPage with a 'buy yarn' helper.
findings.why: Buying the wrong amount of yarn is one of the most common beginner frustrations — too little means a dye-lot mismatch disaster, too much is wasted money. This feature directly serves the target user (beginners) and creates a natural integration point for affiliate links or partner yarn shops in the future.
